questions.prompt: What is the common concept underlying all freestyle maneuvers?
options: Always keeping the motors at full power (throttle pinned)
Managing momentum, that is, channeling the energy of speed and altitude from one form into another
Making the drone as light as possible
Flying only in Angle mode
questions.explanation: Power loops, dives, and split-S all work on momentum: you convert altitude into speed and speed into altitude. Good freestyle isn't pinning the throttle, it's managing energy.

questions.prompt: What is the recommended safe order for learning these three maneuvers?
options: Split-S first, then power loop, then dive
Power loop first, then split-S, then dive
Dive first, then power loop, then split-S
Order doesn't matter, they are all equally difficult
questions.explanation: Dive first (throttle management + recovery), then power loop (its descending half is a dive), and finally split-S (a combination of roll + dive). Each maneuver builds on the skill of the previous one.

questions.prompt: Why is the dive the first maneuver to learn?
options: Because it is the flashiest maneuver
Because it teaches the throttle-management and recovery reflex; these skills are used in the other maneuvers too
Because it requires no throttle at all
Because only the dive is legally allowed
questions.explanation: The dive teaches you to cut throttle in the descent and recover at the bottom. Because the descending half of a power loop is also a dive, this reflex is the foundation of all freestyle.

questions.prompt: How should throttle management work during a power loop?
options: Pinning the throttle in the dive (descending) part
Cutting the throttle completely throughout the whole maneuver
Holding throttle through the climb to roll over the top, then cutting it in the dive (descent) and recovering at the bottom
Throttle should never change, it should stay constant
questions.explanation: You keep momentum through the climb and roll over the top; because the descent is a dive, you cut throttle there and recover gently at the bottom.

questions.prompt: What is the most important safety principle when choosing a spot to learn freestyle maneuvers?
options: The spot should be as small and tight as possible
It should be close to people and obstacles so you can call for help
Having plenty of clearance in every direction (especially up/down), no people/obstacles around, and flying with a spotter
Flying only at night
questions.explanation: Freestyle needs a lot of vertical and horizontal clearance; you need room in every direction to recover. A wide, open area free of people and obstacles, plus a spotter, is the foundation of safety.
